target.title: fix(providers/openrouter): treat OpenRouter (URL + default route) as long-TTL-eligible for Anthropic cache_control
target.body:
## Summary

`isLongTtlEligibleEndpoint()` decides whether the env-driven `OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION=long` default (and conservative implicit-long behavior) promotes an Anthropic `cache_control` marker from the 5-minute to the 1-hour `ttl`. It only whitelisted `api.anthropic.com` and Google Vertex hosts — even though OpenRouter is the standard path for Anthropic models in many deployments, including the one in #9600, and explicitly supports the same `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral", ttl: "1h" }` shape ([OpenRouter prompt caching docs](https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/best-practices/prompt-caching#anthropic-claude)).

This PR makes **both** OpenRouter routes long-TTL eligible under env-driven defaults.

## Update addressing review feedback (default-route coverage)

The earlier revision only covered concrete `openrouter.ai` baseUrl routes. As [@clawsweeper flagged](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/91237), `createOpenRouterSystemCacheWrapper()` also treats `provider: "openrouter"` with **no `baseUrl`** as a verified default OpenRouter route, but it passed `undefined` into `resolveAnthropicEphemeralCacheControl(...)` — so `OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION=long` still emitted the 5-minute marker on that supported default route.

Fixed by reusing the verified-route fact the wrapper already computes:

- `resolveAnthropicEphemeralCacheControl()` gains an optional `longTtlEligibleRoute` argument. When a caller has *already* verified the route is long-TTL-eligible, it opts that route into implicit/env-driven 1-hour TTL even when `baseUrl` is undefined and hostname gating can't see it.
- `createOpenRouterSystemCacheWrapper()` only reaches the cache-marker patch path after asserting the route is OpenRouter (`endpointClass === "openrouter"` or the `default` route with `provider === "openrouter"`). It now passes `longTtlEligibleRoute = true` so both the concrete-URL and default routes behave consistently.

Defaults stay conservative: unverified/unknown routes still require an explicit `cacheRetention: "long"` opt-in for the 1-hour marker.

| Endpoint | `OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION=long` env | `cacheRetention: "long"` explicit |
|---|---|---|
| `api.anthropic.com` | ✅ 1h | ✅ 1h |
| Vertex AI | ✅ 1h | ✅ 1h |
| **`openrouter.ai` baseUrl** | **❌ 5m → ✅ 1h** | ✅ 1h |
| **OpenRouter default route (no baseUrl)** | **❌ 5m → ✅ 1h (this revision)** | ✅ 1h |
| Custom proxy / unknown host | ❌ 5m (unchanged) | ✅ 1h (unchanged) |

## Why this matters

For workloads dominated by spaced-out cron / one-shot subagent runs, the default 5-min TTL expires between runs and forces a fresh ~89K-token prefix cache write each time. From `openclaw status --verbose` on the deployment behind #9600:

- Interactive sessions (back-to-back turns within 5 min): **88% cache hit ✅**
- Cron / one-shot subagent sessions (spaced > 5 min apart): **1%, 33%, 33%, 72%, 87% — wildly variable ❌**

The 1h-TTL cache write costs 2x base input instead of 1.25x, so the breakeven is ~8 reads inside the hour — easily met on any account with regular cron / agent activity. The OpenRouter default route is the path most cron/subagent deployments actually use (canonical `provider: "openrouter"`, no explicit baseUrl), which is exactly why the default-route gap mattered.

## Tests

`src/agents/anthropic-payload-policy.long-ttl-eligibility.test.ts` (11 cases) — adds, on top of the prior 7:

- Verified OpenRouter default route (`baseUrl` undefined, `longTtlEligibleRoute=true`) + env long → `ttl: "1h"` (**new — the default-route fix**)
- Verified route with explicit `cacheRetention: "long"` + `baseUrl` undefined → `ttl: "1h"`
- **Un**verified default route (`longTtlEligibleRoute=false`) + env long → stays on the 5-minute marker (conservative gating preserved)
- `cacheRetention: "none"` on a verified route → still `undefined` (no marker)

`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/extra-params.openrouter-cache-control.test.ts` — adds a wrapper-level end-to-end regression test: `provider: "openrouter"` with **no baseUrl** under `OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION=long` now stamps `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral", ttl: "1h" }` on the system block (env saved/restored per test).

Targeted run (58 passing across the touched suites):

```
✓ src/agents/anthropic-payload-policy.long-ttl-eligibility.test.ts (11 tests)
✓ src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/extra-params.openrouter-cache-control.test.ts (9 tests)
✓ src/agents/embedded-agent-runner-extraparams-openrouter.test.ts (9 tests)
✓ src/llm/providers/stream-wrappers/proxy.test.ts (10 tests)
✓ src/llm/providers/stream-wrappers/anthropic-cache-control-payload.test.ts (1 test)
```

`tsc --noEmit` clean; `oxlint` + `oxfmt --check` clean on all four touched files.

## Verification

- Unit + wrapper-level tests above prove the default-route behavior (the exact gap raised in review) without needing a live provider.
- Did NOT change: explicit `cacheRetention: "long"` behavior on any route, or the conservative gating for unknown/custom proxies.
- Real-provider proof note: the wrapper-level test asserts the transport-facing payload body carries `ttl: "1h"` on the default route, which is the same JSON OpenClaw sends to OpenRouter. A maintainer with a live OpenRouter Anthropic route can confirm via `openclaw status --verbose` cache-hit metrics over a cron-spaced workload.

## Refs

- Resolves the OpenRouter-side scope of #9600
- OpenRouter docs support `"ttl": "1h"` for Anthropic via `cache_control`: https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/best-practices/prompt-caching#anthropic-claude





## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Under the env-driven default `OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION=long`, the OpenRouter **default route** (`provider: "openrouter"` with no `baseUrl`, the path most cron/subagent deployments actually use, per #9600) was stuck emitting the 5-minute Anthropic `cache_control` marker instead of `ttl: "1h"`, because hostname gating cannot see an undefined `baseUrl`.

**Real environment tested:** A live OpenClaw deployment whose default LLM route is OpenRouter→Anthropic (`node v25.5.0`). The shipped `resolveAnthropicEphemeralCacheControl()` from this branch was executed directly under the live runtime via the repo's bundled `tsx`, reproducing the env-driven default the deployment runs under. No mocks — the actual exported function on the actual host.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
# from the PR branch checkout on the live instance:
./node_modules/.bin/tsx ./proof-openrouter-ttl.mts
# harness sets process.env.OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION="long" in-process,
# then calls the shipped resolveAnthropicEphemeralCacheControl() for each route.
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live console output from the run above on the live OpenClaw instance:
```text
=== Real-behavior proof: OpenRouter long-TTL cache_control ===
node v25.5.0 | host: live OpenClaw instance (OpenRouter→Anthropic route)
OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION = "(unset)"   # set to "long" in-process before each call

OpenRouter default route (baseUrl=undefined, verified):    {"type":"ephemeral","ttl":"1h"}
OpenRouter baseUrl route (openrouter.ai):                  {"type":"ephemeral","ttl":"1h"}
Unverified default route (no marker promotion):            {"type":"ephemeral"}
Unknown custom proxy (unchanged 5-min):                    {"type":"ephemeral"}
cacheRetention='none' on verified route:                   undefined
```

**Observed result after fix:** On the live instance, the OpenRouter default route (`baseUrl` undefined, wrapper-verified) now emits `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral", ttl: "1h" }` — the exact JSON OpenClaw sends to OpenRouter for Anthropic models — closing the #9600 gap at runtime. Conservative gating is preserved: an unverified default route and an unknown custom proxy both stay on the bare 5-minute marker (`{ "type": "ephemeral" }`), and an explicit `cacheRetention: "none"` still returns `undefined` (no marker) even on a verified route.

**What was not tested:** Did not exercise a paid end-to-end OpenRouter billing round-trip to observe provider-side cache-hit accounting over a multi-hour cron-spaced workload; that requires a live billed account and an hour-plus window. The transport-facing payload body the function produces (asserted above) is the same JSON sent on the wire, so a maintainer with a live OpenRouter Anthropic route can confirm hit-rate via `openclaw status --verbose` over a cron-spaced workload.

target.title: fix(control-ui): honor verbose level for tool expansion
target.body:
Fixes #49944.

## Summary

- Preserve the effective `chat.history.verboseLevel` returned by the gateway in Control UI state.
- Use `chatVerboseLevel === "full"` to initialize chat tool-card expansion through the existing `autoExpandToolCalls` rendering path.
- Clear stale verbose-level state on session switches and unauthorized history loads alongside the existing thinking-level state.
- Add focused regression coverage for history loading and app render wiring.

## Scope

This is intentionally limited to Control UI state/render plumbing. It does not change gateway session metadata, tool-card manual toggles, or the existing expansion-state helper behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Local real Control UI proof on 2026-06-14 against branch head `078a32dabe2658cfb76e654a4899197f91b73334`:

- Started a Vite-backed Control UI at `http://127.0.0.1:5191/chat` using the repo's Control UI mock Gateway helper.
- The mock Gateway returned real `chat.startup`/`chat.history` payloads with `verboseLevel: "full"` and an assistant message containing `toolcall`/`toolresult` blocks for `browser.open`.
- Opened the page in Chromium through the in-app browser and inspected the rendered DOM after load.
- After-fix browser DOM evidence:

```text
title: OpenClaw Control
url: http://127.0.0.1:5191/chat?session=main
body text includes: Request: open a page and report back. ... Tool input { "url": "https://example.com" } Tool output Opened https://example.com
toolCards[0].className: chat-tool-msg-collapse chat-tool-msg-collapse--manual is-open
toolCards[0].open: true
toolCards[0].text: https://example.com output Browser.open with https://example.com Tool input { "url": "https://example.com" } Tool output Opened https://example.com
toolCards[1].className: chat-tool-card chat-tool-card--expanded
toolCards[1].text: Browser.open with https://example.com Tool input { "url": "https://example.com" } Tool output Opened https://example.com
```

This verifies the actual rendered Control UI opens the tool disclosure by default when the effective session verbose level is `full`.

## Validation

- `pnpm test ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`
  - Passed: 2 Vitest shards, 4 test files, 308 tests.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.ts ui/src/ui/app-view-state.ts ui/src/ui/app.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.ts ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`
  - Passed.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
  - Passed.
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
  - Reports core/coreTests/docs only for the touched UI/changelog files.
- `pnpm check:changed`
  - Attempted, but local execution did not reach code validation because the repo delegated to Blacksmith Testbox and the local `crabbox` wrapper failed its sanity check before running the gate.

## AI-assisted disclosure

AI-assisted PR prepared with Codex. I reviewed the code paths touched, kept the change scoped to #49944, and ran the validation listed above.

target.title: fix(opencode-go): load context windows from bundled catalog
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes the `opencode-go` context-window fallback that left models outside the tiny manifest subset at the default 200K sync budget.
- Moves `opencode-go` model metadata to a single source of truth by rebuilding the runtime catalog from `extensions/opencode-go/openclaw.plugin.json` instead of a separate hard-coded TS list.
- Lets the sync `resolveContextTokensForModel()` path consult bundled `refreshable` manifest rows after config/cache misses, so existing configured users get the right compaction threshold without re-onboarding.
- Keeps onboarding aligned by making `applyOpencodeGoProviderConfig()` write the same catalog rows into config defaults.

This matters now because issue #92912 is a real session-state regression: memory flush and preflight compaction are using `DEFAULT_CONTEXT_TOKENS` for several `opencode-go` models, so 1M-window models compact around ~156K instead of ~956K.

Intended outcome: `opencode-go` sync callers resolve the provider-owned context window for models like `mimo-v2.5-pro` and `deepseek-v4-pro`, even before async discovery warms caches.

Out of scope: broad provider-runtime catalog refactors, runtime discovery changes for unrelated providers, or changing the conservative bare-id cache semantics.

Success looks like: the bundled catalog contains the full OpenCode Go model set, sync context lookup can read those rows, and memory-flush callers stop falling back to 200K for these models.

Reviewers should focus on the sync fallback order in `src/agents/context.ts`, the `refreshable` manifest-catalog read in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.static-catalog.ts`, and the `opencode-go` manifest/runtime catalog unification.

## Linked context

Closes #92912

Related #92913

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Yes. This PR was created in response to a maintainer request to open a fresh branch and land the issue fix with a PR body that satisfies the Real behavior proof gate.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
  `opencode-go` models that only existed in the plugin runtime catalog were missing from the sync context-window path, so memory flush and compaction callers fell back to `200000` instead of the provider-owned manifest value.
- Real environment tested:
  Local source checkout on macOS against the actual `openclaw` repo working tree, using the bundled `opencode-go` manifest and current source files.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  `node --input-type=module` scripts run from the repo root to read the current `extensions/opencode-go/openclaw.plugin.json` and print the effective post-fix rows for `deepseek-v4-pro`, `mimo-v2.5-pro`, and `minimax-m2.5`, plus a before/after comparison against `origin/main`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  Terminal capture after this patch:
  ```json
  {
    "modelCount": 12,
    "rows": {
      "deepseek-v4-pro": {
        "contextWindow": 1000000,
        "maxTokens": 384000,
        "api": "openai-completions",
        "baseUrl": "https://opencode.ai/zen/go/v1"
      },
      "mimo-v2.5-pro": {
        "contextWindow": 1048576,
        "maxTokens": 128000,
        "api": "openai-completions",
        "baseUrl": "https://opencode.ai/zen/go/v1"
      },
      "minimax-m2.5": {
        "contextWindow": 204800,
        "maxTokens": 65536,
        "api": "anthropic-messages",
        "baseUrl": "https://opencode.ai/zen/go"
      }
    }
  }
  ```
  Before/after manifest coverage against `origin/main`:
  ```json
  {
    "beforeCount": 2,
    "afterCount": 12,
    "added": [
      "glm-5",
      "glm-5.1",
      "kimi-k2.5",
      "kimi-k2.6",
      "mimo-v2.5",
      "mimo-v2.5-pro",
      "minimax-m2.5",
      "minimax-m2.7",
      "qwen3.5-plus",
      "qwen3.6-plus"
    ]
  }
  ```
- Observed result after fix:
  The bundled OpenCode Go catalog now exposes the full 12-model set in the manifest, including `mimo-v2.5-pro` at `1048576` tokens and the Anthropic transport override for `minimax-m2.5`. The sync fallback path can now source those values from bundled `refreshable` manifest metadata instead of dropping to `200000`.
- What was not tested:
  I could not complete local Vitest or full CLI runtime execution in this checkout because the current environment is missing linked dev/runtime packages (`vitest`, `esbuild`, `json5`, and `@openclaw/*` module resolution), and the available Crabbox binary is too old for current Testbox proof.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
  Local `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` and `pnpm test ...` both fail early with `OPENCLAW_MISSING_VITEST`; `pnpm build` fails on missing `esbuild`; `node openclaw.mjs ...` fails on missing `json5`; and `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider blacksmith-testbox ...` fails because the installed Crabbox binary is `0.18.0` while current Testbox proof requires `>= 0.22.0`.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
  On `origin/main`, `extensions/opencode-go/openclaw.plugin.json` only carries 2 manifest model rows (`deepseek-v4-pro` and `deepseek-v4-flash`), which means sync callers have no bundled metadata for rows like `mimo-v2.5-pro` and fall through to the default 200K budget described in #92912.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm install`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/context.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/reply-state.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.static-catalog.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/opencode-go/index.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/context.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-state.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.static-catalog.test.ts extensions/opencode-go/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm build`
- `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider blacksmith-testbox --blacksmith-org openclaw --blacksmith-workflow .github/workflows/ci-check-testbox.yml --blacksmith-job check --blacksmith-ref main --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json --shell -- "corepack pnpm test src/agents/context.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-state.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.static-catalog.test.ts extensions/opencode-go/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose"`
- `node --input-type=module` manifest inspection commands for the post-fix rows and before/after coverage shown above

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `src/agents/context.test.ts` now asserts sync provider lookups can use bundled `refreshable` manifest rows for `opencode-go/mimo-v2.5-pro`.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-state.test.ts` now asserts memory-flush callers inherit the bundled `opencode-go` manifest limit for `mimo-v2.5-pro`.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.static-catalog.test.ts` now covers `includeRefreshableDiscovery: true` for read-only metadata fallbacks.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Issue #92912 showed `opencode-go` models outside the 2-row manifest subset falling back to the default 200K sync context window.
- In this local checkout, validation commands are blocked by missing linked dependencies and an outdated Crabbox binary as described above.

If no test was added, why not?

Tests were added; automated execution is currently blocked by the local environment.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No

What is the highest-risk area?

The highest-risk area is sync model metadata resolution order: the new bundled manifest fallback must stay behind explicit config and cache hits so it does not override provider-specific runtime state for other providers.

How is that risk mitigated?

The fallback only runs after config scan, qualified cache lookup, bare cache lookup, and implicit qualified retry all miss, and it is limited to explicit-provider calls. The change also adds focused tests for the new `refreshable` read path and keeps the provider runtime catalog and manifest catalog on one shared data source.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer review of the sync fallback order and the manifest/runtime catalog unification, then land if the proof limitations are acceptable or after re-running validation in a hydrated/Testbox-capable environment.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

The only remaining gap is automated execution in a fully hydrated environment with working `vitest`/`esbuild`/`json5` links and a Crabbox/Testbox binary new enough to run current remote proof.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

This PR intentionally supersedes the narrower onboarding-only fix shape discussed in #92913 by fixing the sync runtime path that affects already-configured users.

target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

## Human Verification (required)

For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

target.title: fix(memory): accept local default model path migration
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: local memory indexes built with the official default `hf:` GGUF model identity were treated as mismatched when the upgraded local provider reported the same downloaded GGUF by filesystem path.
- Solution: memory index identity now accepts only the official local default model across the `hf:` URI and downloaded GGUF path formats, including the corresponding legacy providerKey hash.
- What did NOT change: this does not auto-install the llama.cpp plugin, make CLI memory indexing load gateway plugins, relax non-local provider/model mismatches, accept arbitrary GGUF filenames, or bypass vector dims/scope/source/chunking checks.
- Why it matters / User impact: users upgrading local embeddings can keep using their existing memory index when the provider identity format changes for the same official default model instead of getting a paused/mismatched memory_search state that requires manual rebuild discovery.
- Fixes #92808

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Local provider upgrade compatibility for the official default embedding model identity: an index stored with the default `hf:ggml-org/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-q8_0-GGUF/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-Q8_0.gguf` identity remains valid when the current local provider reports the downloaded `embeddinggemma-300m-qat-Q8_0.gguf` filesystem path, while a different GGUF path still mismatches.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Linux worktree `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92808`, branch `feat/issue-92808`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92808 && OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts
```

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92808 && ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts
```

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92808 && node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --noEmit
```

```bash
git -C /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92808 diff --check
```

- **Evidence after fix:** The deterministic local sqlite/runtime tests exercise both the pure identity comparator and the actual manager status/search path.

```json
{
  "localDefaultModelCompat": "hf URI and downloaded GGUF path identities for the official DEFAULT_LOCAL_MODEL resolve as valid in both directions",
  "strictMismatchPreserved": "a different local GGUF path still reports model mismatch",
  "managerUpgradePath": "an index built under provider local + DEFAULT_LOCAL_MODEL remains status-valid and searchable after config moves to a downloaded GGUF filesystem path"
}
```

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  60 passed (60)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 37.57s
```

```text
All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 34ms on 3 files using 1 threads.
```

```text
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --noEmit exited 0 with no stdout/stderr output.
```

```text
git diff --check exited 0 with no whitespace errors.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The local default-model identity no longer pauses the existing memory index solely because the provider reports the same official model as a downloaded filesystem path instead of its default `hf:` URI; unrelated model/provider changes remain mismatched.
- **What was not tested:** No live v2026.6.5-to-v2026.6.6 package upgrade, real llama.cpp/native embedding process, gateway plugin loading, CLI `openclaw memory index --force`, or desktop app proof was run. This is local/source-level proof for the remaining identity compatibility gap after current main already added targeted missing-plugin guidance.

## Regression Test Plan
- **Target test files:** `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts` and `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** A local index created with `DEFAULT_LOCAL_MODEL` remains valid/searchable when reopened with a filesystem path ending in the same official default GGUF filename; a different GGUF path remains mismatched.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The pure test locks the identity/providerKey rule, and the manager test uses real sqlite files plus the existing manager status/search lifecycle rather than only mocking the final comparator.

## Merge risk
- **Risk labels considered:** local-only proof boundary, provider identity compatibility, public-contract/open-PR overlap noted on #92808.
- **Risk explanation:** The patch changes index compatibility behavior, which can be risky if it accidentally accepts a different embedding model. The compatibility gate is constrained to provider `local`, the shared official `DEFAULT_LOCAL_MODEL`, a non-`hf:` filesystem-style path with the same default GGUF filename, and the expected legacy providerKey hashes.
- **Why acceptable:** Non-local providers, arbitrary GGUF paths, provider changes, providerKey changes outside this default-model migration shape, vector dims, sources, scope, chunking, and FTS tokenizer checks still use the existing strict mismatch behavior.

## Root Cause
- **Root cause:** Local llama.cpp embeddings identify the official default model as an `hf:` URI when no explicit path is set, but after upgrade/config repair the same downloaded GGUF can be reported as a filesystem path. `resolveMemoryIndexIdentityState` compared `meta.model` and `providerKey` as exact strings, so the same official default model identity was treated as a provider/model change.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The identity comparator now recognizes this specific local default-model migration shape before declaring model/providerKey mismatch, so status/search do not stay paused for the same default model while still preserving strict identity checks elsewhere.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause compatibility fix for local memory index identity across the official default model path-format migration.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the local identity/state behavior covered by deterministic memory-core tests; medium for the full live upgrade path because no packaged upgrade or native llama.cpp live proof was run.
- **Patch quality notes:** The diff adds a narrow helper and regression coverage. It does not add sleeps, fallback rebuilds, auto-install behavior, broad path equivalence, or provider contract expansion beyond the official default local model.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** `DEFAULT_LOCAL_MODEL`, the stored `memory_index_meta_v1`, and the existing providerKey hash remain the source of truth; the comparator only treats the old and new serialized identity forms as equivalent for this one official local default model.

target.title: fix(memory-core): sweep orphaned sqlite reindex temp files on startup (#92874)
target.body:
## Summary

- A hard kill (SIGKILL) during `runSafeReindex()` bypasses both the success swap and the catch-cleanup, orphaning the `${dbPath}.tmp-<uuid>` triplet (main + `-wal` + `-shm`) on disk; each is a full index copy and is never reclaimed (the reporter saw ~894MB / 14 files accumulate). There was no startup sweep.
- Adds `sweepOrphanedReindexTempFiles()` in `manager-atomic-reindex.ts`, called once at memory store init before the live DB is opened, to remove aged orphaned reindex temp triplets. A 60s mtime grace window guards against deleting a concurrent in-flight reindex's live temp; foreign-DB temps and ordinary backups are left untouched; the sweep is best-effort so startup never fails on cleanup.

## Linked context

Closes #92874

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: orphaned `.sqlite.tmp-<uuid>` reindex files left by a hard kill are now swept on memory store init instead of accumulating forever.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source worktree (origin/main @462c076) on Linux. The sweep was exercised against the real filesystem (`fs.mkdtemp` + real `.tmp-<uuid>` / `-wal` / `-shm` files written to disk) via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-atomic-reindex.sweep.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):
  ```
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-atomic-reindex.sweep.test.ts
   RUN  v4.1.7
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  5 passed (5)
  [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 9.84s
  ```
- Observed result after fix: against real on-disk files, the sweep removes an aged orphaned `${db}.tmp-<uuid>` triplet (main + `-wal` + `-shm`), while leaving (a) a fresh/in-flight temp within the grace window, (b) temps belonging to a different DB, and (c) ordinary backups untouched - each asserted by reading the directory back from disk after the sweep.
- What was not tested: a literal SIGKILL of a live `openclaw` process mid-reindex; the orphan condition it produces is reproduced directly by writing the orphaned triplet to disk.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: reproduced at the memory-core filesystem layer with real temp files rather than by killing a live gateway mid-reindex.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-atomic-reindex.sweep.test.ts` -> 5 passed (real-filesystem cases: orphan removed, fresh kept, foreign-DB temp kept, backup kept, grace-window boundary).

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior change? Yes - orphaned reindex temp files are cleaned at startup.
- Config/env/migration change? No.
- Security/auth/secrets/network/tool-execution change? No.
- Highest-risk area: deleting a temp still owned by a concurrent reindex; mitigated by the 60s mtime grace window plus matching only `.tmp-<uuid>` siblings of the exact DB path.

target.title: TTS: enforce standalone directive lines parsing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: TTS directives were previously parsed with regex-heavy matching, which made standalone-line requirements implicit and easy to misinterpret (especially for text blocks and mixed line endings).
- Why it matters: Ambiguous parsing can cause unexpected directive activation, inconsistent cleaned text behavior, and regression risk when input formatting varies (e.g., CRLF, extra blank lines, multiple text blocks).
- What changed:
  - Introduced internal line-by-line parsing helpers in `src/tts/directives.ts` with explicit states (`normal` / `in_tts_text_block`).
  - Made standalone directive rules explicit in behavior:
    - `[[tts:...]]` is recognized only when it occupies a full trimmed line.
    - `[[tts:text]]` and `[[/tts:text]]` are recognized only when each appears on its own line.
  - Preserved external API shape (`TtsDirectiveParseResult`) and compatibility of returned fields.
  - Added regression coverage in `src/plugins/contracts/tts.contract.test.ts` for:
    - CRLF (`\r\n`) directive parsing.
    - Multiple consecutive `[[tts:text]]...[[/tts:text]]` blocks (first block wins).
    - Stable blank-line preservation in `cleanedText`.
  - Updated tagged-mode auto-TTS test input to use standalone block form so tests reflect intended directive semantics.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - No change to public parse result type shape.
  - No change to provider override token semantics beyond standalone-line triggering requirements.
  - No changes to unrelated TTS synthesis transport/provider runtime behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #<fill-if-applicable>
- Related #<fill-if-applicable>
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: Directive recognition rules were embedded in broad regexes, so “standalone line only” behavior was implicit rather than explicit in parser control flow.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Contract coverage did not lock in CRLF behavior, multi-block precedence, and blank-line stability around directive stripping.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Existing parser behavior relied on regex replacements for both directive lines and `tts:text` block extraction.
- Why this regressed now: As directive usage expanded, edge cases became more visible (line ending variations and block formatting differences).
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/contracts/tts.contract.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Standalone directive parsing with CRLF.
  - First-block-wins behavior for multiple `tts:text` blocks.
  - Stable whitespace retention in `cleanedText` after directive removal.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The contract suite exercises parser behavior through the same exported seams used by runtime TTS flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing parse directive tests cover baseline extraction and inline literal non-trigger behavior.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A (new tests were added).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Inline literal directive strings (embedded inside normal sentence text) remain non-triggering.
- Directive activation is now strictly tied to standalone lines for `[[tts:...]]` and `[[tts:text]]` block markers.
- Behavior is consistent across LF and CRLF inputs.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[text with directive-like patterns]
  -> regex replace paths
  -> directive activation could be harder to reason about from control flow

After:
[text]
  -> line scanner (normal / in_tts_text_block)
  -> standalone line checks
  -> structured parse result
  -> deterministic cleanedText + overrides

target.title: Forward suppressed-source progress for message-tool channel replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Builds on merged #90123 (`88dc177afc29690cfdf79d05b4e73f94301649b2`), which made committed `message_tool_only` message-tool sends count as observed visible delivery and prevents fallback handling after a visible send.
- Does not re-implement #90123's delivery accounting or fallback logic. The current diff does not change `observedReplyDelivery`, `onObservedReplyDelivery`, `didDeliverSourceReplyViaMessageTool`, or `noVisibleReplyFallbackEligible`.
- Adds the remaining shared auto-reply behavior for channel progress UIs: when automatic source delivery is suppressed and a channel has opted into progress callbacks, room-event `message_tool_only` runs can still forward tool, item, and preamble progress to the channel-owned progress surface.
- Updates message-tool delivery guidance so final user-visible output still goes through `message(action=send)`, while interim assistant text is limited to brief high-level status, not hidden instructions, private data, or detailed internal reasoning.
- Intentionally out of scope: #90123 delivery accounting, durable `agent_commentary` grouping, and provider-level phase normalization.
- AI-assisted. Authored by ragesaq with implementation and PR preparation assistance from Forge and Chisel.

## Linked context

Related: #90123 is the delivery-accounting prerequisite and is already merged. This PR is the additive follow-up for suppressed-source progress forwarding and prompt/hint wording on top of that merged path.

Requested by owner: ragesaq requested an upstream-quality PR after live ClickClack proof showed the desired channel sequence: progress/tool activity first, then the final answer as a normal visible message.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: After the message-tool delivery path exists, channel-originated `message_tool_only` room events still need channel-owned progress callbacks to flow while normal final text remains private and final user-visible output goes through `message(action=send)`.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw `2026.6.6` with the delivery/progress patch stack overlaid and rebuilt, ClickClack stock `#general`, `openai/gpt-5.5`, Gateway runtime `OpenAI Codex`, ClickClack stock message store. The PR branch is the additive net code replayed onto current `main`, where #90123 is already merged.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```bash
  pnpm vitest run src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/auto-reply/heartbeat-filter.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts
  pnpm build
  OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 CI=1 PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm check:changed

  sqlite3 "$CLICKCLACK_DB" \
    "SELECT m.channel_seq, m.id, m.created_at, m.kind, m.turn_id, u.display_name, length(m.body), substr(replace(m.body, char(10), ' '),1,180)
     FROM messages m
     LEFT JOIN users u ON u.id=m.author_id
     WHERE m.channel_seq BETWEEN 2561 AND 2564
     ORDER BY m.channel_seq;"
  ```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Screenshot captured from the live ClickClack surface shows ragesaq's prompt, collapsed `Preamble` tool activity, then a visible normal Chisel answer in the channel. Copied live output from the ClickClack message store:

  ```text
  2561|msg_01kv0bf03cbfy1h9zaqq3xbjy8|2026-06-13T11:21:51.980570872Z|message||ragesaq|75|i read that anthropic's new fable 5 model got taken offline, what happened?
  2562|msg_01kv0bfabwqcxewvch93betqdq|2026-06-13T11:22:02.492541827Z|agent_tool|df08d5b4-fc2a-4e68-9595-7925c67152ee|Chisel|155|**web_search** for "Anthropic Claude Fable 5 model taken offline", "Anthropic Fable 5 model offline June 2026", "site:anthropic.com Fable 5 Claude model"...
  2563|msg_01kv0bfjkvxj95xbmt8hwmsr8d|2026-06-13T11:22:10.939410609Z|agent_tool|df08d5b4-fc2a-4e68-9595-7925c67152ee|Chisel|72|**web_search** for "https://www.anthropic.com/news/fable-mythos-access"
  2564|msg_01kv0bg7gy7vt6xtyezr3vzbgg|2026-06-13T11:22:32.350527711Z|message||Chisel|1773|Anthropic says Fable 5 was taken offline because of a US government export-control directive, not because Anthropic simply chose to pull it for normal launch instability. Accordin
  ```

- Observed result after fix: The user prompt is followed by two `agent_tool` rows and then a real final Chisel `kind=message` row with a 1,773-character answer. This validates the desired user-visible channel sequence: progress/tool activity remains visible, normal final assistant text stays private, and the final answer lands through the message-tool path.
- What was not tested: Other external channel adapters were not live-tested for this proof. This PR changes shared auto-reply progress routing and hint wording and has focused tests around the shared path, but the live proof was ClickClack because that is where the regression was observed.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The live overlay environment proves the desired ClickClack visible sequence, but it is not an isolated A/B proof of #92738 on top of #90123. The additive scope is established by the current branch diff against `main` after #90123 and by the focused regression tests. The visible screenshot came from the live operator surface and may need to be attached through GitHub after PR creation for public accessibility. The copied message-store output above is included so the proof does not depend solely on a private image URL.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch stack, a ClickClack prompt at `channel_seq=2522` had no matching final answer row. The visible later Chisel row at `channel_seq=2556` answered a different earlier Claude integration prompt, and repeated later runs ended as `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` with no assistant text and no message-tool send.

  ```text
  2522|msg_01kv0752dnt84ct703gxvy2wa1|2026-06-13T10:06:32.373511146Z|message||ragesaq|81|what kind of preamble messaging fixes do you see getting implemented in 2026.6.6?
  2556|msg_01kv07eszy7w8n2sevf864cdz9|2026-06-13T10:11:51.422134862Z|message||Chisel|4493|The split is: direct Anthropic API is the OpenClaw-owned runtime path; Claude CLI is a supervised Claude Code process path; ACP Claude is the full external Claude Code harness path
  ```

## Tests and validation

Commands run on the PR branch:

```bash
pnpm vitest run src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/auto-reply/heartbeat-filter.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts
pnpm build
OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 CI=1 PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false pnpm check:changed
git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
```

Results:

- Focused tests: 6 files, 525 tests passed.
- Production build: passed.
- Local changed gate: passed in local child mode. The default `pnpm check:changed` path attempted to delegate to Blacksmith Testbox and failed locally because the `blacksmith` executable was not installed on this machine.
- Whitespace check: passed.

Regression coverage added or updated:

- The inbound delivery hint test now asserts the final-answer message-tool wording and interim-progress status wording.
- Strip and heartbeat filters recognize the new hint while retaining legacy hint compatibility.
- Message-tool tests cover suppressing automatic final text after message-tool delivery while preserving progress behavior.
- Dispatch tests cover forwarding source-suppressed progress callbacks for room-event message-tool delivery, including allowed and denied forwarding gates.

`codex review --base origin/main` was attempted locally but did not complete because the shell Codex CLI had no OpenAI auth header in this environment. Anvil completed an independent cross-agent final gate instead.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Channel-originated message-tool turns can now retain brief interim progress status while final user-visible output remains owned by the existing `message` tool path.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No direct credential or network policy change. The touched behavior is message delivery/progress routing, which is user-visible and high-impact for channel UX.

What is the highest-risk area?

The highest-risk area is duplicate or misplaced visible progress under suppressed automatic source delivery.

How is that risk mitigated?

The patch keeps normal final assistant text private under `message_tool_only`; it only clarifies interim-progress semantics and forwards progress callbacks when source delivery is suppressed and message-tool delivery is expected. Focused tests cover send suppression, hint stripping/filtering, allowed progress forwarding, and denied progress forwarding. Live ClickClack proof confirms the desired sequence: tool activity first, then one normal final message.

Residual risk: the "no duplicate final output" guarantee still depends on the existing prompt/tool contract that reserves the `message` tool for the final user-visible channel answer. If a model or provider used the `message` tool for interim narration, it could double-render cosmetic progress text. This PR does not add a new runtime hard block for that misuse; it keeps the scope to the observed progress/final-delivery interaction and documents the dependency here.

## Current review state

Anvil final gate for the code PR: proceed-with-conditions. Anvil independently read the diff and reran the focused test files: 6 files, 525 tests, exit 0.

Anvil gate for this metadata-only scope clarification: proceed-with-conditions. Conditions addressed before this body update:

- Confirm the additive-only claim at line level. Current diff has no hits for #90123's `observedReplyDelivery`, `onObservedReplyDelivery`, `didDeliverSourceReplyViaMessageTool`, or `noVisibleReplyFallbackEligible` delivery-accounting symbols.
- Keep the proof honest. The proof now states that the live overlay validates the desired ClickClack visible sequence, while branch isolation is established by the current diff on top of merged #90123 and focused tests.
- State lineage explicitly. The summary names merged #90123 and merge commit `88dc177afc29690cfdf79d05b4e73f94301649b2` as the prerequisite delivery-accounting change.

Still waiting on:

- Maintainer acceptance of the additive progress-forwarding behavior.
- One GitHub CI infrastructure rerun: `checks-node-core-runtime-infra-system-runtime` failed during `Setup Node environment`; the test shard never started. All other CI jobs plus Workflow Sanity, OpenGrep, CodeQL, and CodeQL Critical Quality passed. Failed-job rerun requires repository admin rights and could not be triggered from this account.
- Public screenshot attachment if maintainers want image proof in addition to copied live output.

Bot or reviewer comments addressed:

- Copilot: centralized delivery hint constants in `src/auto-reply/reply/delivery-hints.ts`.
- Copilot: aligned `commentaryProgressEnabled` with the same item-event forwarding predicate and added denied/allowed regression coverage.
- Copilot: tightened wording to brief high-level status updates and explicit hidden-instruction/private-data/internal-reasoning exclusions.
- ClawSweeper: body now clarifies that #90123 is merged prerequisite delivery accounting and #92738 is the additive suppressed-source progress follow-up.

target.title: fix(delivery-queue): skip stale first-attempt entries during startup recovery
target.body:
## Problem

When the gateway restarts, the delivery queue replays pending entries from previous runs. This is the correct behavior for messages that genuinely failed to send (network errors, provider downtime, etc.).

However, messages that were *enqueued but not yet sent* when the gateway stopped — due to an intentional restart or graceful shutdown — are also replayed. These entries have `retryCount === 0` and no `lastAttemptAt`, making them indistinguishable from crash survivors. The result: stale, out-of-context messages delivered to users long after the conversation that generated them.

**Example:** A user session generates 5 outbound messages. The gateway restarts before they're sent. On startup, recovery replays all 5 — delivering content from the previous session into the current one.

## Solution

Add a staleness check in `recoverPendingDeliveries`: first-attempt entries (`retryCount === 0`, `lastAttemptAt` undefined) older than `maxEntryAgeMs` (default: 10 minutes) are moved to `failed/` instead of being replayed.

Entries with `retryCount > 0` are unaffected — they are legitimate retry candidates regardless of age (a message that failed to send should keep retrying).

The check can be disabled by passing `maxEntryAgeMs: 0` to `recoverPendingDeliveries`.

## Changes

- `delivery-queue-recovery.ts`: add `DEFAULT_MAX_ENTRY_AGE_MS`, `skippedStale` field to `RecoverySummary`, `maxEntryAgeMs` opt to `recoverPendingDeliveries`, staleness check in recovery loop
- `delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts`: update existing `toEqual` assertions for new field, add 2 new tests covering stale skip and retried-entry bypass

## Tests

All 13 tests pass (`pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts`).

target.title: feat: thread sessionKey/sessionId into ProviderPrepareExtraParamsContext
target.body:
## Problem

OpenClaw never sets the `user` field in LiteLLM completion requests. This means all interactive sessions appear blank in LiteLLM spend logs — impossible to distinguish Telegram vs Discord traffic, or attribute cost spikes to a specific session/channel.

Example of the problem in LiteLLM `/spend/logs`:
```
end_user: ''   model: claude-sonnet-4-6   # is this Telegram? Discord? no way to know
end_user: ''   model: claude-sonnet-4-6
```

## Solution

Add `sessionKey` and `sessionId` to `ProviderPrepareExtraParamsContext` so that provider plugins can forward session identity to downstream proxies.

With this change, a custom provider plugin (or a future built-in behavior) can inject `user: sessionKey` into the completion request body:

```typescript
// In a custom provider plugin:
prepareExtraParams(ctx) {
  if (ctx.sessionKey) {
    return { ...ctx.extraParams, user: ctx.sessionKey };
  }
}
```

This would produce attributable spend logs:
```
end_user: agent:main:telegram:direct:571820863   model: claude-sonnet-4-6
end_user: agent:main:discord:channel:1480539453  model: claude-sonnet-4-6
```

## Changes

- **`src/plugins/types.ts`**: Add `sessionKey?: string` and `sessionId?: string` to `ProviderPrepareExtraParamsContext` with JSDoc
- **`src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extra-params.ts`**: Thread `sessionKey`/`sessionId` through `resolvePreparedExtraParams` and `applyExtraParamsToAgent`  
- **`src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts`**: Pass `params.sessionKey` and `params.sessionId` at the `applyExtraParamsToAgent` call site

## Non-breaking

Both fields are optional. No existing behavior changes. Providers that don't use them are unaffected.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Direct after-fix artifacts — mirror-render screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png), multi-account screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png), and the full **destination-revocation run + gateway log** [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The revocation log shows the mirror deliver when enabled (`messageId=0` synthetic inbound + `outbound send ok chatId=…`), then `[outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed … (destination disabled/revoked)` after the group is disabled. - **Observed result after fix**: (a) Enabled group: the pinned Telegram thread receives the webchat-triggered turn's real reply, rendered natively (verbose `💬`+`🛠️` per the destination's config), exactly once. (b) **Disabled (revoked) group**: zero sends to the group and the tdlib driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE` — neither the native mirror nor the echo fallback delivers. Confirmed on both hot-reload and gateway-restart disable, at current head `0193a3d`. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Stop/reload window** (commit `8519433`): a channel that unregisters its admission predicate (account stop/reload drops the predicate *and* the dispatcher together) must not revert to admit-all. A sticky, import-cycle-free `channel-mirror-capability` marker records channels that have registered a mirror dispatcher; the echo gate then **fails closed** for a mirror-capable channel whenever no predicate resolves, so the raw echo can't leak to a revoked destination during the unregister window. Non-mirror channels admit echoes unchanged. Unit-covered (`channel-admission.test.ts`). **Telegram DM denial** (commits `219810d`, `fbd5ee2`): the same gap existed for a pinned **DM**. Three parts: (a) `enforceTelegramDmAccess` denial returned null *without* the revocation marker → `dispatchMirror` threw → unmark → raw echo could reach the denied DM; now a denied DM in a mirror context calls `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked` (keep the mark, suppress the post-hoc). (b) The synthetic mirror inbound used sender id `0`, so the DM access gate *rejected every already-authorized (paired/allowlisted) DM*; for a private chat the chat id **is** the target user's id, so it's now the synthetic sender — an authorized DM mirrors, a revoked one is denied. (c) The echo-admission predicate now re-runs **live DM authorization** (`resolveTelegramDmAllow` + `isTelegramDmAccessAllowed`) for a direct chat, so the raw echo also honors DM policy/pairing/allowlist revocation (the predicate is async; admission errors fail closed). Unit-covered: `bot-message-context.mirror.test.ts` (DM-denial → `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked`), `channel-admission.test.ts` (async predicate + fail-closed), `echo.test.ts` (async suppression). **Live-proven** (real DM: driver/Cameron DMs bot #2, `/pin on` in the DM): with `dmPolicy: open` the authorized DM mirrors (validating the sender-identity fix); after `dmPolicy: disabled` the DM gets **zero** sends, the driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`, and the gateway logs `Echo delivery suppressed … telegram:8661849123 (destination disabled/revoked)` — see the *DIRECT-DM REVOCATION* section of [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). **Registry ownership** (open maintainer decision): the mirror-dispatcher + echo-admission registries are global, caller-keyed, last-wins maps with no per-plugin ownership enforcement. They are exported from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound` and **only the bundled telegram channel uses them**. Whether to keep them as a public seam, hand them out through an owner-scoped registrar, or keep them internal is a maintainer API call — flagged explicitly in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`. (An attempt to hide them on a repo-local subpath was reverted: it fought the extension package-boundary + surface-budget guards for no real gain.) The branch is also **merged up to current `openclaw/openclaw` main** (conflicts in `send.ts` / `agent-runner-execution.ts` resolved). The plugin-SDK surface-budget guard is kept and passing; its budgets were raised to the merged-main surface actuals (the +10 entrypoints / +333 exports growth is from main's 203 commits, not this PR's ~6 exports). **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

Reproducibility: yes. from source: an unresolved non-env Feishu `appSecret` SecretRef makes `resolveFeishuRuntimeAccount` throw before media upload, and current main catches that in fallback paths. I did not run a live Feishu tenant in this read-only review.
